---
title: "Audience & Age-Targeting"
aliases: ["Age Banding", "Writing for an Age Group", "Reading Level"]
family: "Story, Character & Voice"
media: [interactive-fiction, live-game, tabletop]
facets:
  effects: [reach, trust]
  problems: [safety-failure, voice-drift]
  components: [narrative-primitives, roles-and-social-frame, state-and-resource-trackers]
  genre: [childrens-ya]
provenance: "researched"
---

*Silhouette: Pitching the prose and the stakes to a reader of a known age without ever talking down to them.*

<!-- SPECIMEN-TAGS:START (generated by scripts/specimen_tags.py — do not edit by hand) -->
::: {.specimen-line effect="reach" problem="safety-failure" media="if,live,tabletop" family="story"}
:::
<!-- SPECIMEN-TAGS:END -->

A seven-year-old and a seventeen-year-old do not read the same way. A piece written for one will misfire for the other. The difference is not only in the hard words. It is in how long a sentence can run before the reader loses the thread. It is in how far ahead a consequence can sit before they forget what caused it. It is in how much darkness the story can carry before it stops being theirs. Knowing the reader's age changes nearly every decision you make on the page.

This is the calibration discipline, and it runs underneath every genre. A horror story for nine-year-olds and a horror story for adults share a genre and almost nothing else. The genre craft of writing for the young, with its young-investigator roles and its rules about who gets agency, is [Children's & YA Conventions](childrens-and-ya-conventions.qmd). This article is the layer below that: the dial you set for any audience of any genre, so the prose and the stakes both land at the same reader.

The trap that swallows beginners is condescension. Writing simply is a craft skill. Writing down is an insult the reader feels before they can name it. Younger readers feel it fastest of all.

## The Mechanism (How it works)

### The age bands and what moves across them

Publishing sorts young readers into bands that are not arbitrary. Each one names a cluster of reading ability, attention span and emotional readiness that hangs together in practice. The boundaries blur at the edges and a strong nine-year-old reads past her band, but the bands still describe real shifts in what a reader can take in. [researched]

Several things change together as you move up the bands. Vocabulary widens from common concrete words to the full range. Sentences lengthen. The protagonist ages, because readers gravitate toward characters a year or two older than themselves, a pull the trade calls "reading up". [researched] Content boundaries loosen, so that violence, fear, death, and romance each get more room. And theme handling deepens, from problems that resolve cleanly toward moral situations with no clean answer. [corpus]

| Band | Reader age | Sentence length | Protagonist | Content ceiling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Picture book | 2–7 | very short, one idea each | any age, often a child or animal | very gentle: mild scares that resolve, no real harm [corpus] |
| Early reader | 5–8 | 5–10 words, simple structure | a touch older than the reader | gentle: slapstick, no graphic threat [corpus] |
| Middle grade | 8–12 | 10–20 words, some complexity | 10–13, on the older edge | genuine stakes; death handled with care; crushes, nothing physical [corpus] |
| Young adult | 13–18 | full adult range | 16–18, not yet at university | real horror and loss; romance to about a kiss [corpus] |
| Adult | 18+ | full range | any | no limit beyond the law; content warnings replace ceilings [corpus] |

The single most useful rule in the table is the protagonist's age. Pin it slightly above the top of your target band and the "reading up" pull does the rest, pulling the youngest readers up toward the character rather than leaving the oldest ones bored. [researched] An eight-year-old hero rarely carries a middle-grade book; a twelve- or thirteen-year-old does. [researched] Push the protagonist into university and a young-adult book quietly becomes a new-adult one, a bridge category for the eighteen-to-twenty-five reader facing first jobs and first independence. [corpus]

Note what the table does not do: it does not pair simple language with simple ideas. That pairing is the error. A middle-grade reader can follow a twisty, demanding plot in plain sentences. The band sets the *prose register and the content ceiling*, not the intelligence of the story underneath. How those young protagonists are handed real power over outcomes, the agency that the young audience demands, is the genre concern in [Children's & YA Conventions](childrens-and-ya-conventions.qmd).

### Reading-level metrics and where they stop being useful

Prose difficulty can be put to a number, and editors do. The Flesch Reading Ease score runs from 0 (hardest) to 100 (easiest). The related Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level maps the same passage onto a US school grade. [researched] Both work from two inputs only: average sentence length and average syllables per word. A score of 6.0 means a text an average sixth-grader reads comfortably. [researched]

The school systems use richer scales. Lexile and ATOS each place a text and a reader on one shared scale, so a 700L book and a 700L reader are a match by construction. [researched] These were built to grade whole books against measured reading ability, which the Flesch formulas were not: Flesch-Kincaid was designed in the 1970s for US Navy technical manuals and is not meant to score a whole novel. [researched]

The limit on all of them is the same, and it is worth stating plainly. The formulas count surface features and nothing else. They cannot see prior knowledge, dialect, irony, or whether a short sentence is clear or merely curt. [researched] They are gameable, too: a string of nonsense short words scores as easy. [researched] So treat a metric as a smoke detector, not a thermostat. A passage that scores three grades above your band is worth a second look. A passage that scores on target can still confuse the reader for reasons no formula registers. Sample real passages and trust the read over the number. Never sacrifice the story to hit a score. [corpus]

::: {.callout-warning}
### The Hazard
Writing down is the failure that hides inside good intentions. It comes in two shapes. The first keeps adult concepts and only shrinks the words, so "the man did a bad thing and felt sad about it" carries grown-up moral weight in a register that cannot hold it. [corpus] The second is moralising. Here a lesson stops the story so a character can teach it, a move children catch faster than any other flaw. [corpus] The fix for both is the same. Lead with story and let the meaning surface on its own. Trust that young readers handle far more complexity than adults credit them with. The tell that you have slipped is a character who exists only to deliver a point. Another tell is a sentence you would be embarrassed to read aloud to the child it is meant for. [synthesis]
:::

### Content boundaries and the gatekeeper

Each band carries a content ceiling. That ceiling moves with the reader rather than the genre. Violence climbs from slapstick to real consequence. Fear climbs from a scare that resolves safely to full horror. Death moves from absent or gentle, usually a pet or a grandparent in middle grade, to lasting and load-bearing in young adult. Romance climbs from crushes to about a kiss, with anything explicit waiting for the adult and new-adult shelves. [corpus] Age-appropriate scares are their own small craft, shared with [Horror Conventions](horror-conventions.qmd), where the question is how to frighten without harming.

One structural fact shapes everything below the adult line. The buyer is often not the reader. A parent or a teacher picks the book for a child, so writing for minors means writing for two audiences at once. The child reads it; the adult has to approve it first. [corpus] This is not a reason to sand the work flat. It is a reason to be deliberate about where the ceiling sits and to signal it plainly. For adult work the ceiling gives way to the content warning. The warning does the same protective job from the other direction. A good one is specific rather than vague ("contains graphic violence" beats "mature content"). It comes before the reader has committed any real time. It warns without spoiling. [corpus]

### The interactive turn: calibrating choice, not just prose

Interactive forms add a dimension the novel does not have, because the reader acts as well as reads. Every band then calibrates the *choices* as well as the prose, and the two have to move together. A young reader who can follow simple sentences can also only hold a simple decision. That means fewer options, with consequences that arrive almost at once and almost no dead ends. [corpus] Raise the band and the branching widens. Consequences can now be delayed across the whole story. Failure states become acceptable and even worth reaching. [corpus]

| Band | Choices per decision | Consequence reach | Failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Early reader | 2 | immediate | avoid; redirect to success [corpus] |
| Middle grade | 3–4 | a few passages ahead | limited, as a learning beat [corpus] |
| Young adult | full | the whole story | weighty, with real cost [corpus] |
| Adult | no limit | the whole story | as the story requires [corpus] |

This is the same scope-and-branching budget the structure family tracks, read through an age lens: the depth a reader can hold is itself an audience variable, so [Branching Narrative Craft](../structure-pacing/branching-narrative-craft.qmd) cannot be settled without first naming the reader. The protagonist's voice has to hold the chosen register too, plain or full, across every branch a player can reach, which is the cross-band application of [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd) and the continuity problem of [Voice & Register Consistency](voice-and-register-consistency.qmd). Keeping the register steady, neither drifting up into adult diction nor down into baby talk, is a [Voice & Register Consistency](voice-and-register-consistency.qmd) discipline applied to a fixed reading level rather than to a character.

Live and physical formats run the same calibration in space rather than prose. The dominant case is the family escape room. Here a parent-and-child team plays together. The adult handles complex text the child cannot. The design has to hand both of them a real job. [corpus] ThinkFun's Escape the Room boxed series bands its titles openly. Stargazer's Manor is rated 10+ (and often cleared with a seven-year-old in the group); Murder in the Mafia is 13+. The gap between them is mostly reading complexity and how deep the deductions go. [corpus] The practical rule that follows is concrete. A group with children under ten needs at least one tactile task per scene that the child can lead alone. Counting items or matching symbols will do. The task has to be load-bearing rather than busywork. [corpus] That layered design is the all-ages strategy in miniature. A surface engages the youngest while a deeper layer rewards the oldest, the same trick a Pixar film runs when it plays one story for the child and another for the adult beside them. [corpus] Calibrating the bands of a mixed-age live group, rather than a single reader, runs into [Mixed-Ability Groups & Universal Design](../players-social-dynamics/mixed-ability-groups-and-universal-design.qmd); the access half of the same problem, where a reading level is a barrier to be lowered, is [Accessibility in Live & Interactive Games](../players-social-dynamics/accessibility-in-live-and-interactive-games.qmd).

::: {.callout-note}
### The Specimen
Pixar's features are the clearest working model of all-ages calibration, and the method is layering rather than compromise. The surface story carries the youngest viewer on plot and feeling alone. A second layer of reference and emotional weight rewards the adult, and the humour is built to land differently at each level rather than splitting the difference. [corpus] The discipline worth stealing is what the layering refuses to do. It plants no in-joke that shuts the child out, and it pitches no theme so mature the child is lost or so thin the adult is bored. An all-ages piece satisfies no one perfectly, by design. It earns its breadth by giving every age a real reason to stay. [corpus]
:::

## Where this fits

**The bigger picture.** Audience and age-targeting is the setting you choose before most other choices in this family make sense. Voice, content, branching depth, and theme handling all read differently once you name the reader, and a piece pitched at the wrong reader fails no matter how good its parts are. It depends on nothing upstream, so it comes first; almost everything downstream depends on it. It is the audience-calibration discipline that genre craft sits on top of, and it spans every genre rather than belonging to any one of them. Get the band right and the reader will trust you enough to follow you anywhere the band allows. Get it wrong by writing up past them or down at them, and they feel the mismatch before they can name it. They will not stay.

**Next steps.** Once the band is set, the genre layer for young readers is [Children's & YA Conventions](childrens-and-ya-conventions.qmd), which turns these settings into the specific craft of writing for the young. To hold the chosen register steady across a whole branching piece, the discipline is [Voice & Register Consistency](voice-and-register-consistency.qmd), and the protagonist's own distinctness within that register is [Character Voice](character-voice.qmd). For the sentence-level prose and point-of-view choices that a reading level constrains, work through [Prose Patterns & Point of View](prose-patterns-and-point-of-view.qmd). And where the audience is a mixed-age room rather than a single reader, the calibration becomes a spatial one in [Mixed-Ability Groups & Universal Design](../players-social-dynamics/mixed-ability-groups-and-universal-design.qmd).

<!-- BACKLINKS:START (generated by scripts/backlinks.py — do not edit by hand) -->

## Referenced by

- [Children's & YA Conventions](childrens-and-ya-conventions.qmd)
- [Jubensha](../../exemplars/jubensha.qmd)
- [Naming Craft: Characters & Locations](naming-craft-characters-and-locations.qmd)
- [Player Types & Casting](../players-social-dynamics/player-types-and-casting.qmd)

<!-- BACKLINKS:END -->
